summary: Evans reflects on the safety and responsibility implications of using machine learning in production systems. She distinguishes between simple, interpretable models and complex ones like random forests, noting that complex models can perform well but make unpredictable mistakes. She argues that being responsible for a system you don't fully understand is both scary and interesting. The post offers practical mitigation strategies like human review, matching model complexity to error tolerance, and using ML insights to improve simpler systems.
key_points: Model interpretability exists on a spectrum — a 10-variable regression is understandable, a large random forest is not
Complex ML models are simultaneously powerful and dangerous because their mistakes are unpredictable and embarrassing
ML models are only as good as their training data, and bad data leads to bad decisions
Practical safety strategies include human oversight, complexity-appropriate model selection, and distilling ML insights into simpler systems
money_quotes: Being responsible for the accuracy of a system that you don't fully understand is scary/ very interesting.
A model you don't understand is awesome. It can perform really well, and you can save time at first by ignoring the details.
A model you don't understand is scary. It will make unpredictable and sometimes embarrassing mistakes. You're responsible for them.
Often when I train a new model I think at some point 'NO PLZ DON'T USE THAT DATA TO MAKE DECISIONS OH NOOOOO'
Use complicated models when it's okay to make unpredictable mistakes, simple models when it's less okay.
Use ML for research, learn why it's doing better, incorporate your findings into a less complex system.
key_insight: The responsible use of machine learning requires matching model complexity to your tolerance for unpredictable mistakes, and using human oversight and interpretability as safety nets.
